main_summary.summary: Robert McClary, interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries on March 9, 2013 in Albany, Georgia, recounts his entry into the Albany Movement and his fieldwork with Charles Sherrod and SNCC across Southwest Georgia. Born at Phoebe Putney Hospital, McClary describes a childhood shaped by his father's physical confrontation with a white man in South Carolina and the family's subsequent migration. He recalls early schooling in the segregated South and describes how local desegregation pressure pulled him into organizing. With Sherrod he worked on tenant rights, welfare-denial appeals, and a plan to organize roughly nine Southwest Georgia counties to elect Black officials locally. He recounts being attacked while sleeping in a SNCC office and notes a fellow staff member's intervention before police removed the attacker. He references a school-board confrontation in a small Southwest Georgia town and the eventual election of its first Black mayor. Closing the interview, he says he felt more hopeful and excited than afraid. The transcript is heavily degraded by automatic-speech-recognition failure; many passages survive only as fragments and topical anchors rather than continuous testimony.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing in Southwest Georgia
main_summary.historical_significance:
McClary's testimony documents grassroots SNCC field work in Southwest Georgia under Charles Sherrod's leadership, including the multi-county voter and electoral strategy and the day-to-day tenant and welfare-appeals casework that sustained the project between mass-meeting moments. His firsthand account of an assault inside a SNCC office, and of a staff colleague intervening before police removed the attacker, adds a primary-source data point to the record of physical risk borne by local field staff.

The interview's value as a primary source is constrained by severe automatic-transcription degradation: large portions of the recording survive in the transcript only as repeated phrases, fragmentary cues, or unrecoverable runs. The anchored facts above are corroborated through audit-confirmed corrections against ground-truth references and the Library of Congress canonical archive, but extended narrative reconstruction from this transcript is not advisable without returning to the source audio.

chapters.title: Born at Phoebe Putney
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, on March 9, 2013, Robert McClary recalls being born on October 19th at Phoebe Putney Hospital, his mother dying when he was young, and his father, who worked a job on the river, moving what was left of the family.

chapters.title: Learning to Walk at Seven
chapters.summary: McClary describes the family falling into poverty after his mother's death, being raised with a sister and brother by his father, learning to walk at about seven years old, the trouble over whether he would go to school, and how the men were paid every two weeks for their work.

chapters.title: He Knocked the White Man Down
chapters.summary: He recounts his father, who had come up from South Carolina, being insulted by a white man and knocking him down in a confrontation, and remembers attending a two-room school with a football field he had to cross.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Southwest Georgia
chapters.title: A New School and the Albany Movement
chapters.summary: McClary describes a high school newly built for Black families, the first families chosen to begin integrating, an incident involving a young white boy and a Black girl, and the early stirring of what became known as the Albany Movement before he himself got involved.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Southwest Georgia
chapters.title: Charles Sherrod and Nine Counties
chapters.summary: He tells of writing for the movement paper and meeting Charles Sherrod, the plan to organize roughly nine counties across Southwest Georgia and take over a county to elect local people, and joining the effort, before the interview pauses for a technical delay.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Southwest Georgia
chapters.title: Tenants and Welfare
chapters.summary: After the recording resumes, McClary describes the everyday work he did with Charles Sherrod: helping tenants and people who were denied welfare, filing appeals when benefits were taken away, and the white residents who had largely left the area.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Southwest Georgia
chapters.title: The Casino Cafe Closed Its Doors
chapters.summary: He recounts an attempt to desegregate a cafe in his hometown, remembered as a casino, where Black customers were directed to a back window, an activist who had come down from New York joined the effort, and the owner closed the place rather than serve them at the front door.

chapters.title: Attacked in the Office at Night
chapters.topic: A Night Raid and Its Aftermath
chapters.summary: McClary describes being attacked while sleeping at the movement office, a man forcing his way in during the night, his escape and the police taking the man away that same night, and afterward learning skills like typing as part of the project.

chapters.title: Self-Defense and the Board
chapters.summary: Working for the Southwest Georgia project, McClary reflects on self-defense as something he would not retire from, and recounts a confrontation tied to a local board in a small town.

chapters.title: More Hopeful Than Afraid
chapters.topic: A First Black Official and His Father
chapters.summary: He recalls helping people meet challenges and the town electing its first Black official, says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid, points to his father as a man who would not back down to white intimidation, and the interview closes with thanks.

loc_video.caption: Robert McClary oral history interview conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
